title: Artifacts
subtitle: Not just an answer in the chat, but a ready-made document, page, or mini-app

sources.title: Claude Features: Artifacts — Claude Help Center

theory.text: When an answer in the chat is inconvenient

theory.md:
Ask Claude to write a long article, a workout table, or a simple game — and picture the result as an ordinary message in the chat. Long text drowns in the conversation, after a couple of edits three versions appear in a row, and it's no longer clear which one is final. And you can't run the game's code in a chat at all — only read it.

For results like these — "works of output" — claude.ai has **Artifacts** — a separate window next to the chat where the document, page, or app you're creating lives.

theory.good: "Create an interactive healthy-eating cheat sheet: a separate page, at the top — the 5 main principles as cards, below — a daily calorie calculator where I enter weight, height, and age. Calm, green colors."
theory.explanation: The first request gives a paragraph of text in the chat — read and forgotten. The second explicitly asks for an interactive page with a concrete structure — Claude will create an app artifact you can actually use, and then refine: "make the cards brighter," "add a mode for athletes."

theory.md:
A finished artifact doesn't have to stay inside your chat. You can **publish** it — get a link that opens the artifact for anyone in a browser, with no software to install. That's how a teacher shares a multiplication-table trainer with the class, and how you send a friend an interactive plan for a trip together.

Two things are important to remember:

1. Publishing means sharing: don't publish artifacts with personal data or work information not meant for others' eyes.
2. You can take a copy or export of the artifact for yourself — copy the text, download the result, and use it outside claude.ai.

Typical publishing scenarios: an interactive cheat sheet for colleagues, a trainer for students, a calculator for clients, a quiz for a party. In every case the recipient simply opens the link — they don't need a claude.ai account and can't see your conversation, only the artifact itself.

The help center at support.claude.com (the Claude Features section, articles on Artifacts) describes artifacts as a separate window next to the chat for self-contained content — documents, code, diagrams, web pages, and simple interactive apps — which can be refined as the dialogue goes on and published via a link.

Artifacts and projects get along great: in the "Studies" project, ask "make interactive flashcards from the terms in my notes" — Claude will take the terms from the project's knowledge base and build a trainer artifact. One request — a ready learning tool.

tasks.question: How does an artifact differ from an ordinary answer in the chat? Select all correct options.
options: It lives in a separate panel next to the chat, not in the message feed
It can be refined — Claude updates the same artifact with new versions
It can be interactive: buttons, calculators, and quizzes work right in the browser
It's always automatically visible to all claude.ai users
tasks.explanation: An artifact is an object in a separate panel, with versions and possible interactivity. But there's no automatic publicity: an artifact becomes available to others only if you publish it yourself and share the link.
tasks.hint: The first three statements are described in the theory. Think about what happens to an artifact until you yourself press "publish."

tasks.question: Which request will almost certainly lead to an artifact being created?
options: "How many calories are in a banana?"
"Make an interactive quiz of 10 questions on film history with score tracking"
tasks.explanation: A quiz with score tracking is a self-contained interactive product, and the word "interactive" signals it directly: an artifact is needed. Short factual questions get an ordinary answer in the chat — an artifact is pointless for them.
tasks.hint: Look for the request whose result is "a thing that gets used," not a line of text.

tasks.instruction: Simulation: ask Claude to create an artifact — a habit tracker (a page or mini-app where you can mark habits by day). Then refine it with one edit.

steps.claudeReply:
Creating an artifact! 🎯 A panel has opened to the right of the chat: **"Habit Tracker"** — a weekly table, rows are habits (exercise, reading, water), columns are days. Clicking a cell places a check mark, and the completion percentage for the week is counted at the bottom.

It's an interactive app: the cells are clickable right in the browser. What should I refine? You can add your own habits, change colors, or add monthly statistics.
steps.failHint: Ask to create a concrete thing, for example: "Make an interactive habit tracker — a page where I mark habits by day of the week."

steps.claudeReply: Done — I updated the artifact, this is already **version 2**. Notice how it works: I didn't recreate the tracker from scratch, I changed the existing one. You look at the result and say what to fix — that's how, over a few iterations, a draft becomes a tool built for you. And if an edit doesn't land — you can go back to the previous version.
steps.failHint: Name a concrete edit: "Add a 'meditation' habit and make completed cells green."

tasks.successMessage: You've gone through the full artifact cycle: creation via an explicit request → refinement via "change this here." That's exactly how documents and mini-apps are built in the real claude.ai.

tasks.instruction: Notes about artifacts. One statement is wrong — find it.
lines: 1. An artifact opens in a separate panel next to the chat.
2. An artifact can be a document, a page, code, or a mini-app.
3. To change an artifact, you have to delete it and start the chat over.
4. A published artifact can be shared via a link.
5. The words "interactive," "separate document" in a request increase the chance of getting an artifact.
tasks.explanation: The error is in line 3: nothing needs to be recreated. The main power of artifacts is iteration: you write "change this here" in the same chat, and Claude updates the artifact, keeping the version history. The other statements are correct.

tasks.instruction:
Mission: create and refine your own artifact.

1. Open **claude.ai** and start a new chat.
2. Ask for an interactive thing for your life, for example: "Create interactive flashcards for memorizing 10 B1-level English words" or "Make a tip calculator page with a percentage slider."
3. Wait for the artifact to appear in the panel next to the chat. Click around on it — make sure it works.
4. Make at least two edits in turn: first a substantive one ("add 5 more words"), then a visual one ("make the cards bigger, background dark").
5. If you like the result and there's no personal data in it — find the publishing option in the artifact panel, get a link, and open it in another browser tab.
checklist: I explicitly asked for an interactive artifact, not just text
The artifact appeared in a separate panel and works when clicked
I made a substantive edit, and the artifact updated
I made a visual edit in a separate message
I published the artifact (or deliberately chose not to) and understand that the link makes it available to others
